attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the cardiology clinic with a six-month history of progressive shortness of breath. He has severe mitral regurgitation and left ventricular dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 25%. He has a medical history of ischaemic cardiomyopathy and atrial fibrillation. His medications include bisoprolol 10 mg daily, spironolactone 50 mg daily, furosemide 80 mg twice daily, apixaban 5 mg twice daily, sacubitril/valsartan 97/103 mg twice daily, and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily. He has a cardiac resynchronisation therapy defibrillator in situ.

On examination, he appears short of breath at rest. His pulse rate is 75/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. Cardiac auscultation reveals a grade 4/6 pansystolic murmur maximal at the apex radiating to the axilla. Chest examination reveals bibasal crackles. There is mild pitting oedema to the ankles bilaterally.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has severe mitral regurgitation with significant left ventricular dysfunction that remains symptomatic despite optimal medical therapy including maximum doses of guideline-directed medical therapy (bisoprolol, spironolactone, sacubitril/valsartan, and dapagliflozin) and cardiac resynchronisation therapy. The next appropriate step in management would be consideration of intervention to address the mitral regurgitation.

Transcatheter mitral valve repair (MitraClip) is an appropriate option for patients with severe mitral regurgitation who remain symptomatic despite optimal medical therapy and who are at high surgical risk. This procedure involves placing a clip to bring the anterior and posterior mitral leaflets together, reducing regurgitation while avoiding the risks of open surgery. While the primary goal is to reduce mitral regurgitation and improve symptoms, studies have shown variable effects on ejection fraction. The main benefits are symptom improvement, reduced hospitalisations, and potentially improved survival, rather than improvement in ejection fraction.

Increasing furosemide dose would only provide temporary symptomatic relief without addressing the underlying pathology. The patient is already on optimal medical therapy with maximum doses of evidence-based medications, and escalating diuretics alone is unlikely to provide sustained benefit.

Heart transplant assessment would be a more aggressive approach and would typically be reserved for younger patients with end-stage heart failure who have exhausted other treatment options. The presence of other comorbidities and age may make transplantation less suitable.

Surgical mitral valve replacement carries significant risks in patients with severe left ventricular dysfunction and multiple comorbidities. The perioperative mortality would likely be prohibitively high in this case.

Palliative care referral would be premature at this stage as there are still active treatment options available that could improve symptoms and potentially survival. Palliative care involvement may be appropriate later if interventions are unsuccessful or declined.
learningPoints: Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (MitraClip) is an option for severe mitral regurgitation in patients with high surgical risk.
Optimal medical therapy and cardiac resynchronisation therapy should be trialled before considering interventional procedures.
Left ventricular dysfunction with severe mitral regurgitation carries a poor prognosis without intervention.
references: Praz F, Borger MA, Lanz J, Marin-Cuartas M, Abreu A, Adamo M, Ajmone Marsan N, Barili F, Bonaros N, Cosyns B, De Paulis R, Gamra H, Jahangiri M, Jeppsson A, Klautz RJM, Mores B, Pérez-David E, Pöss J, Prendergast BD, Rocca B, Rossello X, Suzuki M, Thiele H, Tribouilloy CM, Wojakowski W; ESC/EACTS Scientific Document Group. 2025 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease. Eur Heart J. 2025 Aug 29:ehaf194. Available from: https://www.escardio.org/Guidelines/Clinical-Practice-Guidelines/Valvular-Heart-Disease

Stone GW, Lindenfeld J, Abraham WT, Kar S, Lim DS, Mishell JM, Whisenant B, Grayburn PA, Rinaldi M, Kapadia SR, Rajagopal V, Sarembock IJ, Brieke A, Marx SO, Cohen DJ, Weissman NJ, Mack MJ; COAPT Investigators. Transcatheter Mitral-Valve Repair in Patients with Heart Failure. N Engl J Med. 2018 Dec 13;379(24):2307-2318. Available from: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1806640
